claim.hypothesis: Hypothesis 1a: Relaxation during the weekend is positively associated with joviality.
claim.hypothesis_location: Section: Recovery and Affect; Subsection: Relaxation and affect; p. 1142.
claim.statement: ...relaxation was signiﬁcantly related to positive affective states at the end of the weekend and at the end of the following workweek… relaxation signiﬁcantly predicted joviality (b¼0.16, p <0.05, Time 2; b¼0.15, p <0.01, Time 3…).
claim.statement_location: Section: Results; Subsection: Relaxation and affect; p. 1148.

data.source: Self-constructed surveys sent out by the researchers
data.notes: Only about 50% finished and returned all surveys (229 is the final sample, post-non-response).

method.description: The authors ran individual level surveys asking about weekend routines and affect and then used hierarchical regression analyses to study the effect of relaxation in the previous weekend on joviality of the subsequent weekend.
method.steps: (1) Recruit German preschool teacher participants; (2) Sent survey packages to the departments for each participant which included instructions, surveys, and stamped return envelopes; (3) Participants filled out the surveys in accordance with the instructions and mailed them back directly to the researchers; (4) Used hierarchical regression analyses to examine the effect of relaxation on joviality.
method.control_variables: Demographic variables (gender, age, having children, work hours per week, workdays per week) and level of each outcome at Time 1

results.summary: Relaxation was signiﬁcantly related to positive affective states at the end of the weekend and at the end of the following workweek.

metadata.original_paper_title: The weekend matters: Relationships between stress recovery and affective experiences
